w:r.w:t: MMA Career RPG · Browser-Based · Single-Player MVP
Confidential — For Internal Development Use Only

Ground & Pound: Chronicles of the Cage is a browser-based, text-driven MMA career RPG. You start as an unknown amateur with nothing but raw potential and a gym membership, and you fight your way through five fictional promotion tiers to the apex: the Global Combat Series (GCS) world championship.

The game is built around one compelling fantasy — building a fighter from zero, making every training decision, picking your fights carefully, managing your body, and eventually standing in the biggest cage in the world with a belt. Every mechanic serves that fantasy.

The MVP is single-player only. Multiplayer is scoped for v2.0. The energy system is the core pacing and monetisation mechanism — players always have something meaningful to spend Energy on, and purchasing Energy Packs is never blocked by timers.
Ground & Pound: Chronicles of the Cage
18–40; MMA fans; sports management fans; idle/incremental RPG players
Single-player career mode; 5 promotion tiers; full fight and training systems; no PvP
Scoped for v2.0 — gym rivalries, online rankings, live matchmaking
Progression is driven entirely by Overall Rating — no separate XP/level bar
No time gates on fights; limits are daily fight caps and stat economics
Every design decision in this document was made against three rules. When mechanics conflict, these rules resolve the conflict in order.

Rule 1 — Energy Packs must always have somewhere to go. No timer, no cooldown, no gate may block a player who has Energy from spending it on something meaningful. The game's monetisation lives or dies on this rule.

Rule 2 — Progress must be earned, not purchased. Buying Energy Packs lets you do more per day. It never lets you skip the opponent quality curve. A player who skips training and spams fights will get beaten up by players who trained. Money buys time, not power.

Rule 3 — No separate level bar. The Overall Rating is the single progression number. It is derived from stats. It is visible, comparable, and meaningful. Adding a second XP bar creates confusion and dilutes the stat system.

Most games gate progression with locked doors: 'you need level 20 to enter this zone.' Ground & Pound uses a softer, more interesting gate: you can go wherever you want, but your opponents will reflect your readiness. Rush to Regional Pro at Overall 28 and you will lose consistently to fighters at Overall 45. The losses teach the lesson. The game never needs to say 'you cannot proceed' — it just makes proceeding painful if you haven't prepared.

The only hard gates are the signing fees (Iron cost to join a new promotion) and the Overall minimums for gym access. These are structural, not arbitrary — you can't train at an Elite gym if you're not an elite fighter yet. The gym quality gates the stat ceiling, which gates the Overall, which gates the gym. The loop is self-consistent.
Fighter Identity — A knockout artist's career plays nothing like a grappler's. Style choice shapes every outcome.
Meaningful Decisions — Training allocation, opponent selection, strategy, and Odd Jobs all carry real consequences.
Authentic Progression — The path mirrors real MMA. You earn nothing; you build everything.
Session Richness — At every stage there are 4–6 different Energy-spending activities available. Sessions never feel like just clicking the same button.
Respect for Spending — A paying player gets more sessions per day. They never get a stat advantage over a patient free player.
Character creation is permanent except for the nickname (changeable once free, then costs Iron). Every choice shapes the fighter's statistical identity and opening narrative.
First name, last name, and an optional nickname displayed as: Marcus "The Mauler" Reed. The nickname appears in fight announcements, news blurbs, and leaderboards.
Four weight classes are available at launch. Weight class determines the entire opponent pool, gym ecosystem, and promotional ladder. Changing weight class later resets promotion tier standing.
The most popular division; deepest NPC roster; hardest title path

Primary style sets starting stat distribution and grants one passive ability. Total starting stat budget is always 100 points across all 8 stats — no style starts stronger overall, just differently shaped.
Combination Puncher: 3+ strike combos deal +15% damage
High-Low Mix: alternating strikes reduce guard faster
Level Change: takedowns cost 1 less Stamina per round
Submission Flow: failed subs reset 50% faster
Clinch King: clinch work deals bonus damage and drains Stamina
Hip Toss: successful throws deal bonus landing damage
Combat Sambo: equal bonus to submission and ground-and-pound
Unpredictability: opponent accuracy reduced 10% vs. you
+2 to all 8 stats (balanced boost — total +16, best overall start)
+25% XP from all training sessions permanently
Punching power and accuracy; affects damage and KO probability
Strike speed, evasion, reaction time; reduces opponent accuracy vs. you
Kicking effectiveness; leg kicks drain Stamina; head kicks carry KO threat
Takedown offence and defence; cage control positioning
Damage from top position; posture control on the ground
Submission offence and defence — applying and escaping chokes and locks
Durability vs. strikes; how much punishment before KO/TKO
Meta-modifier: improves all stat effectiveness by a small % per point; hard to train
4.2 Overall Rating — The Single Progression Number
There are no levels. There is no separate XP bar. The Overall Rating is the only progression number that matters.

Overall is a weighted average of all 8 stats. The weighting favours the player's primary style stats slightly, so a Boxer's Overall leans on STR/SPD/CHN while a Wrestler's leans on WRE/GND. This means two fighters at Overall 55 can have very different actual stat profiles — which is exactly what makes matchups interesting.
Weighted average of all 8 stats. Primary style stats: ×1.2 weight. Secondary: ×1.0. Off-style: ×0.85.
Shown on fighter card, visible to all. Ranges from ~12 (new player) to ~92 (GCS champion).
Gym tier unlock thresholds; promotion signing eligibility; opponent matchmaking; player comparison
It is not a level. It does not gate fights directly. A player can always accept any fight — they just might lose badly.
Grade A–F on fighter card; tells opponents your stand-up threat
Grade A–F; tells opponents your ground threat
Percentage; affects late-round fight commentary and KO probability
Fully restored at fight start; partial recovery between rounds
Spent on every action in a fight; depletion reduces all stat effectiveness
Does NOT recover between rounds; recovers between fights with rest
Damage taken; 0 = KO or TKO; carries across the full fight
This is the engine that governs how long everything takes and why spamming Energy Packs cannot break the game. Understanding this model is essential for all balancing decisions.
Every training session awards 10 XP base to the trained stat. Gym tier multipliers and specialty bonuses apply on top. The XP required per stat point scales steeply:
Noticeable slowdown; specialisation starts to matter
Real commitment needed; sessions feel meaningful
Weeks of focused work per point
Months; every point is an achievement
Cannot be trained — earned through fight milestones only

📌 Getting one stat from 1 to 100 requires roughly 110,000 XP total — approximately 11,000 training sessions on that stat alone. This is physically impossible to brute-force even with heavy Energy Pack spending. Specialisation is not just encouraged — it is mathematically required.

The gym you train at hard-caps how high any stat can go. When a stat hits the cap, further training sessions on that stat return 0 XP. The message is clear: 'you've hit the ceiling here — get a better gym.' The gym unlock is gated by Overall Rating, creating the core progression loop.
+×0.25 on all stats; unique training perks
Stats 96–100 earned through career milestones only

📌 The T1 cap of 35 means a player who only trains STR will hit a wall around STR 35 and start capping. To unlock T2, they need Overall 33 — which means raising other stats too. The system naturally encourages balanced development without forcing it.
5.3 Fight XP — Learning Inside the Cage

Training sessions are not the only way stats grow. After every fight, the stats you actually used receive a Fight XP Bonus. This means fighters who fight frequently still progress — just through a different channel, and usually more narrowly than a well-trained fighter.
Smart fighters learn most from losses

This is why rushing to a higher tier without training is viable as a strategy — but produces a narrow fighter. Someone who wins 30 regional fights without training will have high STR/CHN but terrible WRE/SUB. The stat profile tells the story of how you fought.
5.4 Starting Stat Profiles — Example by Style

📌 All styles start at the same Overall (~14). No style has an early game advantage. The differences become pronounced only as stats develop — a Boxer at Overall 50 fights very differently from a Wrestler at Overall 50.

Energy is the player's action currency. It is entirely separate from in-fight Stamina. It governs how many activities a player can perform per session and is the primary monetisation lever. The fundamental promise: if you have Energy, you always have something meaningful to spend it on.
1 Energy per minute (server-side only; cannot be client-manipulated)
100 minutes from zero (free player); ~120 min for Premium cap
Training sessions, fights, Odd Jobs, media events, doctor visits, weigh-ins, gym travel, scouting
Best XP per session; small injury risk (3%)
Reduces injury timer; no stat XP
Moving to a different gym location
Treating injuries; mandatory after KO loss
Reduces weight cut risk for next fight
Reveals opponent stat highlights before accepting fight

There are no cooldown timers. Instead, each promotion tier has a daily fight cap — a hard limit on how many fights a player can complete in a single 24-hour calendar day (resets at midnight). Energy Packs can accelerate training and jobs between fights but cannot bypass the daily cap.

📌 *Title fights are the only remaining time-based limit. One title defence per real week. This is narrative justification (a championship defence is a major event) and not a monetisation blocker — the Energy spent on camp still rewards Energy Pack purchases.
Why the Daily Cap Works Better Than Cooldowns
A cooldown tells a player 'come back in 12 hours — your session is over.'
A daily cap tells a player 'you have 3 more fights available today — keep going if you want.'
The difference is profound for engagement and monetisation:
→ Players with Energy Packs can do all 8 amateur fights in one long session.
→ The Energy spent on camps between those fights is where the real monetisation sits.
→ Players who hit the cap with Energy left have training, jobs, and media to spend on.
→ No Energy Pack purchase is ever wasted waiting on a timer.
One extra training session or a partial camp top-up
Full camp session for a Regional fight
Full regen equivalent; most popular single purchase
Best value for daily players; part of Premium
One free Case (130 Energy) per day
Title fight mandatory rest halved (narrative rest, not a timer gate)
One free Recovery session per week
Gold fighter card border; Premium profile badge
One free nickname change per month
Early access to new weight classes and features post-launch
Energy Packs restore Energy only — they cannot purchase XP, Iron, or stats directly
All stat gains come from training sessions and fight XP — not from any purchasable item
A free player who plays consistently achieves the same career outcomes as a Premium player — just more slowly
The cosmetic store contains no gameplay items whatsoever

The gym is home base. It determines your stat ceiling, your training XP efficiency, your access to specialist coaching, and the quality of sparring partners available. Choosing the right gym — and eventually upgrading — is the central strategic decision of the career.
7.1 Gym Tiers & Stat Caps (Repeated from Section 5 for reference)
Community centre basement. Real equipment is donated. The coach believes in you.
Dedicated MMA gym. Real coaching. The start of a professional environment.
Full-time facility. Video analysis. Sports nutritionist on staff.
World-class. Champion-level training partners. Sports science team.
The best gym in the world. You train alongside GCS title contenders.
Available T1+; most common early session
Requires T2+ gym or Muay Thai/Kickboxer style
Trains two stats at reduced efficiency each
Highest XP; 3% injury risk per session
T3+ gym required; Fight IQ growth
Speeds up injury recovery; no stat XP
Takedown defence +8% after completing gym storyline
Leg kick damage +10% permanently after 30 sessions
Full camp prep bonus; injury risk −15%
FIQ trains 50% faster; film study unlocked at T2

Every gym has a side quest chain of 3–5 steps. These are required for some tier advancement gates and produce permanent bonuses. They cannot be rushed — each step requires a specific activity, creating meaningful Energy sinks with narrative payoff.
Permanent +3 to primary style stat cap at this gym
Spar 5 times; survive 1 injury without early discharge
Train specialty stat 20 times; hit soft cap
5 film study sessions; win a decision fight
500 ⊗ bonus per fight while enrolled here
10 fights at National+ level; reach top-10
All stats to 60+; complete 5 GCS fights
Legend training mode: +20% XP all sessions

Fights are text-resolved. The player makes strategic decisions before and during the fight; the outcome is determined by stats, strategy, and a seeded RNG that keeps results unpredictable but fair. A player never loses because of bad luck alone — but they can win despite bad odds if the build is right.
Fights are not freely chosen from a menu. The AI promoter generates 3 fight offers at a time. The player picks one. This models real matchmaking.

📌 The Hard offer is the fastest path up the rankings and the fastest path to a losing record. Experienced players develop a feel for when to take the risk and when to build momentum with safer fights first.
After accepting a fight, the player builds a camp. Each Training Camp Action (TCA) is one training session. Minimum recommended camp determines fight performance — skipping camp is legal but costly.
Wait for openings; precise efficient strikes
Take down; smother; punish from top
Systematic leg damage; target the base
Each round resolves server-side. The player sees detailed text commentary round by round. Between rounds, the player chooses a corner instruction.
1. Initiative roll: SPD vs. SPD — determines early positioning advantage
2. Position established: stand-up, clinch, or ground — driven by strategy clash and WRE
3. Action sequence: 4–8 action beats per round — aggressor, action type, outcome
4. Stamina drain per action — both fighters tire through rounds
5. Damage calculation: stat × modifier − opponent defence × RNG variance (±15%)
6. KO/TKO check: triggered when Health drops below 25; probability scales with CHN and hit damage
7. Submission check: if ground and SUB attempted, escape probability = attacker SUB vs. defender SUB
9. Corner instruction: Rest / Push / Target body / Target legs — each shifts next-round modifier by ±10%
Best result for Notoriety and momentum
No win or loss on record
Mental Reset activity required before next fight
After any loss, the next fight enters Comeback Mode. This is not a stat buff — it is a reward multiplier and narrative acknowledgement:
1.5× XP on the comeback fight regardless of outcome
1.3× Iron purse on the comeback fight
Win a comeback fight: Resilience badge added to fighter record (Legacy Score contributor)

Fights going to decision are scored by three independent virtual judges, each with a slight preference bias (striker-friendly, grappler-friendly, or balanced). Each round is scored on aggression, effective striking, effective grappling, and cage control. This produces realistic split and majority decisions — not just 'player wins or loses.'
Missing weight costs 20% of fight purse and creates a Notoriety penalty. Repeated misses attract negative press. A nutritionist (3 Energy) reduces risk by 10% before a fight.
Possible TKO if doctor stops fight
1 fight rest; Iron cost at doctor
Cannot spar; −2 CHN temporary; mandatory rest (1 fight)
Doctor visit required before next fight

📌 Injury probabilities: sparring = 3% per session; fights = 12% minor, 4% major. Fight IQ reduces both. Injuries create tension without being punishing — they are manageable events, not career-enders.

Five tiers define the career arc. Each has its own brand, opponent quality, pay scale, and daily fight cap. Movement between tiers requires meeting ALL gate conditions — but the gates are Iron-based and stat-based, never time-based.

Players can attempt to join a higher promotion at any time if they meet the Iron signing fee and the minimum Overall. The Overall floor is a soft nudge — not a hard lock. A player at Overall 30 could try to skip to National if they saved the Iron. They will get beaten consistently. The losses are the gate. The game trusts the player to figure this out, and losing repeatedly is more memorable than a locked door.
Local Fight League, Community Cage Series
Apex Regional Fighting, Iron Cage Events
National Combat League, Strike Force Championship
Top-5 national ranking; T3 gym access; 2 gym quests complete
Finish required OR exceptional AI promoter discretion
Top-3 GCS ranking; T4 gym access

📌 Signing fees are the primary Iron sink that makes Odd Jobs meaningful. A player who ignores jobs and only fights will stall at the Amateur→Regional gate because they simply don't have the 2,000 ⊗ to sign. This is by design.

Within each tier, fighters are ranked by a Ranking Score calculated from win/loss record weighted by opponent quality, finishing rate, activity, and upset multipliers. The ranking is visible on the player's profile and drives fight offer quality.

The Contender Series is a special annual one-night event. Six hand-picked fighters compete; only finishes earn a GCS contract. Decision wins may earn a contract at the AI promoter's discretion — but a fighter who can only win decisions will be passed over. This creates a dramatic one-fight pressure point that the entire National circuit builds toward.

Your fighter is broke at the start. The gym needs paying, gear needs buying, and no one's handing you a cheque yet. Odd Jobs are the bridge between 0 Iron and your first real equipment. They are Energy sinks with Iron payouts, thematically tied to the MMA world, and available from Day 1.

They serve a second purpose: keeping the player occupied between training sessions and fights when the daily fight cap is reached. A player who has done all 4 of today's Regional Pro fights still has Energy burning and a Notice Board full of jobs to run.

The Notice Board at the gym refreshes every 2 hours with 4–6 available jobs. Some jobs rotate by career stage — you don't get offered TV commentary at Amateur tier. The board gives players a reason to check back between sessions without being a hard time gate.
Tiny XP to trained stat; rare: minor injury introduction
Gym lore revealed in flavour text
Small Notoriety gain; named NPC fan chance
Next fight accept costs 3 Energy instead of 5
Reduces next weight cut difficulty by one step
Major Notoriety boost; sponsor unlock trigger
10.3 What Iron Buys — The Item Economy
Iron is the in-game currency earned from fights and jobs. It is spent on gym fees, equipment, medical bills, and signing fees. Here is the full purchase landscape by career stage:
First real gear purchase; 2-day goal for active job player
Consumable; one active at a time
+5% XP all sessions at this gym
+20% XP on training for 2 weeks (duration item)
Unlocks enhanced opponent scouting (full stat reveal)
+10% all training XP; injury risk −8% permanently while active

All equipment has a Durability rating (0–100). It degrades 3–8 points per fight and 1–2 per sparring session. Below 20 durability, the stat bonus halves. At 0, the item is broken. Repair costs 20% of purchase price. This creates a steady Iron drain throughout the career — even rich GCS fighters need to maintain their gear.
The complete list of activities available between fights. At any point in the game, a player with Energy should have at least 4–6 meaningful things to do. This section documents all of them.
Boosts fight purse 10% for next fight
Legacy Score bonus; one-time per career
Before each fight, the coach presents one of five scenarios. The player chooses a response. No Energy cost — pure strategic flavour:
Stay calm / Respond aggressively / Walk away
Calm: +5 CHN for fight. Aggressive: +5 STR −5 CHN. Walk: +3 FIQ.
Training partner injures you in camp
Cancel sparring / Push through / Reduce intensity
Cancel: −1 TCA. Push: injury risk + full XP. Reduce: half XP, 0 risk.
Accept: fight immediately with reduced camp. Decline: no penalty.
Push forward / Conserve / Target body / Target legs
Shifts fight resolution modifier for next round ±10%
Each affects Notoriety differently; honest answer may seed rival narrative
After 2 fights against the same NPC opponent, a Rivalry flag triggers. The rivalry opens a short activity chain leading to a Grudge Match with elevated rewards:
Trash talk exchange — 1 Energy; +100 Notoriety; seeds rematch narrative
Rival scouting — 3 Energy; reveals rival's current full stat profile
War of words — 4 Energy; press quote boosting both fighters' Notoriety
Grudge match — The rematch: +20% XP; +15% purse; unique commentary lines
Rivalry resolved — Win for a Rival Defeated badge on profile and Legacy Score bonus

Three Daily Missions generate each calendar day. Completing all three awards a Daily Bonus Pack: 20 Energy + 500 Iron. Missions are always achievable with the free daily Energy budget — they are never gated behind purchases.
Raise any stat by 1 point today
Win your next fight by finish
Log in 3 days in a row
A themed bonus week rotates every 7 days, adding variety to the grind:
Training Week — All training sessions +20% XP
Fight Week — All fight purses +25%; best time to schedule fights
Sponsor Showcase — Sponsor quest progress counts double
Recovery Week — All injury recovery ×2 speed
Grudge Night — Any grudge match fight earns ×3 XP
These trigger automatically based on player behaviour. They cannot be ignored — certain next actions are blocked until resolved:
Weight Spike — Inactive for 3+ real days. Must complete 2 S&C sessions before next fight offer.
Overtraining — 8+ training sessions in 24 real hours. −10% XP on all sessions for 48 hours. Fix: 1 Recovery session.
Mental Fatigue — 3 consecutive losses. Mental Reset activity (3 Energy: film study + coach talk) required before accepting fight offers.
Injury Flare-up — Pre-existing injury has 15% chance to flare during camp. Doctor or accept camp quality penalty.
12. Fighter Profile & Overall Rating in Practice

The Fighter Profile is the player's public identity. It is the single screen where all progress is visible at a glance. In v2.0 multiplayer, it becomes the comparison surface — the first thing another player checks before challenging you.
Fighter card — Name, nickname, weight class, age (in-game; starts 22, advances with career)
Record — W-L-D displayed prominently; sub-records for KO, Sub, and Decision
Overall Rating — Large, prominent number. The single progression indicator.
Stats panel — All 8 stats as a radar chart plus individual values
Win/Loss ratio — Displayed alongside Overall for comparison with other players
Derived ratings — Striker Rating and Grappler Rating as letter grades
Career highlights — Scrollable log of significant results with fight narratives
Titles held — Belt icons for any championships won

The two numbers that define a fighter in the eyes of the world — and in v2.0, in the eyes of other players — are Overall Rating and Win/Loss record. These are always displayed together. A player at Overall 72 with a 45-8 record tells a different story than a player at Overall 72 with a 20-5 record. The record reflects how you fought your way there; the Overall reflects what you've become.
Derived from weighted stats. Ranges ~12 (new) to ~92 (GCS champion). The primary progression number.
Fights won ÷ total fights. Displayed as a percentage and as W-L-D record.
% of wins by KO, TKO, or submission. A high finish rate at a high Overall is the mark of a dangerous fighter.
Position within current tier. Driven by win/loss + opponent quality + activity.

Fighters age in-game. One real-world week = one in-game month. The fighter starts at age 22 (player-chosen 22–28). As they age, training diminishing returns become slightly steeper and injury recovery lengthens. Peak years are 25–33. After 35 in-game, a Retirement Clock appears — the fighter can still compete but the narrative begins acknowledging the twilight.
This creates urgency. Players feel pressure to achieve goals within a career window rather than grinding indefinitely. It also makes the Legacy Score (calculated at retirement) feel genuinely earned.

On retirement, Legacy Score is calculated from championships won and defended, finish rate, opponent quality, Notoriety peak, career length, and activity. The score is permanent — it lives on a Hall of Fame wall. Starting a new fighter grants a small Legacy Bonus: a cosmetic title in the intro and a minor starting stat boost representing accumulated player knowledge.

Everything in this section is derived from the stat XP curve, energy economics, and tier gate requirements documented above. These are realistic estimates — not targets or guarantees. Individual playstyle, win rate, and Energy Pack spending will shift these timescales.
First win; first gear purchase; Coach's Test started
10 wins; 2,000 ⊗ saved; ready for pro debut
First pay; first sponsor; gym upgrade; first injury
Top-10 ranking; regional title shot; Prospect Notoriety
T3 gym; top-5 ranking; first main event; grudge match
Everything on the line; one night
Top-10 GCS; TV appearance; major sponsor
Top-3 GCS; interim title; T4 gym
Title win; defences; Hall of Fame path
5 defences; Hall of Fame; Legacy Score peak
Buy Energy Packs and train non-stop
Stat soft caps enforce a ceiling based on gym tier. T1 gym = max 35 in any stat. No XP awarded beyond cap regardless of sessions.
You can — but fight XP alone builds a narrow, unbalanced fighter. NPC opponents at higher tiers expose weaknesses immediately. The losses are the wall.
Signing fee requires 2,000 ⊗. Without Odd Jobs, you don't have it. Even if you somehow do, Overall 28 vs. Overall 42 opponents = consistent losses.
Skip to National via win streak
15 pro wins required, plus top-10 regional ranking, plus 10,000 ⊗ signing fee. The win count alone takes real sessions.
No such item exists. GCS contract is awarded for winning the Contender Series or reaching the qualification threshold organically.
8 GCS fights required + top-3 ranking + Overall 72+. 8 GCS fights at 1/day = 8 real days minimum. Overall 72 requires sustained elite training.
The game world is populated by AI fighters with names, nicknames, styles, records, and personalities. They feel like real rivals, not placeholder content.
Simple AI; basic styles; teaching-mode opponents
Defined styles; some signature moves; trash talk flavour
Story arcs; grudge triggers; narrative quotes
High drama; personality-rich; memorable one-night rivals
Deep bios; signature techniques; championship narratives
The Brawler — High STR/CHN; low FIQ; relentless pressure; fades late
The Technician — High SPD/FIQ; precise and efficient; rarely flashy but always effective
The Grappler — High WRE/GND; seeks takedown immediately; controls position
The Submission Specialist — High SUB; passive on feet; baits to ground
The Tank — Extreme CHN; absorbs punishment; wins by attrition
The Finisher — High variance; explosive; gets KO'd too but always exciting
The Dark Horse — Uneven stats; wins via FIQ and heart; upsets the favourite
Daily cap feels arbitrary at amateur
Frame as 'your body needs recovery' with clear narrative text; 8 fights is genuinely a lot for one day
Stat grind feels slow mid-career (40–60 range)
Weekly Training events boost XP; gym quests provide burst rewards; fight XP keeps stats moving even on low-training days
Iron economy breaks — players too rich or too poor
Signing fees scale sharply; gear durability creates constant spend; gym monthly fees drain at all tiers
500+ commentary lines; contextual templates; personality-driven NPC flavour; grudge match unique lines
Clear in-game communication: 'Energy Packs = more sessions. Never more power.' Parity metrics tracked and published.
GCS has 25-fighter roster; title defences; Hall of Fame path; Legacy Score extends indefinitely
In-game currency. Earned from fights, jobs, and sponsorships. Spent on gear, gym fees, signing fees, and medical bills.
Player action resource. 100 cap (120 Premium). 1 pt/min regen. Spent on all activities.
In-fight resource. 100 per fight. Depleted by actions within a fight. Not the same as Energy.
The single progression number. Weighted average of all 8 stats. Replaces levels entirely.
Training Camp Action. One training session allocated to fight preparation.
Fight IQ. The meta-stat that improves overall combat effectiveness across all disciplines.
Career prestige score. Affects pay, media invitations, and sponsorship unlocks.
Calculated on retirement. Summarises the career. Enables new game+ bonus.
Global Combat Series. The fictional world's top MMA promotion. The end goal.
Annual one-night event. Win to earn a GCS contract.
The maximum a stat can reach at a given gym tier. 0 XP awarded beyond cap.
Post-loss state. Next fight earns 1.5× XP and 1.3× Iron purse.
Gym interface showing available Odd Jobs. Refreshes every 2 hours.
📌 Game Plan strategy, equipment, and camp quality can shift these by up to ±15%. Upsets are always possible. That's MMA.
Average sessions per active player per day: 2–3
Average fights per active player per day: 1–4 (varies by tier and Energy budget)
Monthly paying player rate: 10–15% of MAU
Free vs. paid career completion parity: tracked quarterly; gap must not exceed 15% on time-to-title metric
